activities_projects.title: The SSS vs. SAS Verification
activities_projects.objective: Distinguish between SSS and SAS criteria through construction
steps: Construct triangle ABC with AB=5cm, BC=6cm, and AC=7cm
Construct triangle PQR with PQ=5cm, QR=6cm, and ∠Q=60°
Verify if triangle ABC can be placed exactly over PQR
Explain why they may or may not be congruent
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes how different criteria affect congruence

activities_projects.title: Hidden Congruent Triangles
activities_projects.objective: Identify congruent triangles within a larger polygon
steps: Draw a perfect rectangle and its two diagonals
Identify the four smaller triangles formed inside
Prove which pairs are congruent using the SSS or SAS rule
Mark corresponding parts with matching colors
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies congruence in complex geometric figures
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of congruent pairs (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Ambiguous Case (SSA)
activities_projects.objective: Understand why Side-Side-Angle is not a congruence rule
steps: Draw a line segment AB = 5cm and an angle of 30° at A
Use a compass set to 3cm to draw an arc from B that intersects the 30° line at two different points
Construct two different triangles from these points
Discuss why having two sides and a non-included angle doesn't guarantee a 'twin'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the limitations of congruence criteria
activities_projects.evaluation: Successful construction of two different triangles (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: ASA and AAS Comparison
activities_projects.objective: Explore the relationship between ASA and AAS rules
steps: Draw triangle XYZ where XY=5cm, ∠X=50°, and ∠Y=70°
Calculate the third angle ∠Z using the angle sum property
Construct another triangle using the AAS rule (side XY and angles Y and Z)
Show that AAS is effectively a variation of ASA
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the flexibility of angle-based congruence

activities_projects.title: RHS Proof in Isosceles Triangles
activities_projects.objective: Apply RHS congruence in symmetrical shapes
steps: Draw an isosceles triangle ABC where AB=AC
Draw a perpendicular line (altitude) from A to the base BC at point D
Compare triangle ABD and triangle ACD
Prove they are congruent using the RHS rule
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies RHS criteria to prove properties of isosceles triangles

activities_projects.title: The Mirror Image Challenge
steps: Draw an scalene triangle on graph paper
Place a mirror along one of its sides
Draw the reflection seen in the mirror
Prove the original and the reflection are congruent 'twins'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Connects congruence with the concept of symmetry
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the reflected drawing (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Congruent Partitioning
steps: Fold a square paper to divide it into 4 congruent triangles
Label the vertices and use CPCT to show they are identical
Repeat the process to divide a rectangle into 2 congruent trapeziums
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Develops spatial reasoning and partitioning skills
activities_projects.evaluation: Precision of folds and labeling (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Constructing Twins from Data
activities_projects.objective: Construct triangles given specific congruent parts
steps: Given: Base=6cm, Median=5cm, and one angle=45°
Try to construct two different people's triangles using this data
Verify if all students' triangles are congruent or if more data was needed
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understand what constitutes 'sufficient data' for congruence
activities_projects.evaluation: Analysis of uniqueness of the triangle (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: CPCT Application Project
activities_projects.objective: Use Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles to find unknown values
steps: Draw two triangles and state they are congruent by SAS
If side AB=x+5 and side PQ=12, find the value of x
If ∠A=2y and ∠P=80°, find the value of y
Show the working clearly
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Uses algebraic methods to solve congruence problems

activities_projects.title: Geometric Twins flow-chart
steps: Create a decision tree/flow-chart
The chart should start with 'Are three parts equal?'
Branch out into SSS, SAS, ASA, and RHS with diagrams for each
Include a 'Stop' branch for AAA and SSA
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes the entire chapter into a logical map
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity and completeness of the flow-chart (10 marks)
